nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reefs and lagoons over sand and rubble margins of algal reefs, sometimes near seagrass beds.

diet.notes: Wild diet is dominated by filamentous algae, harpacticoid copepods, ostracods, and other small infaunal crustaceans gleaned by sifting mouthfuls of sand through the gills. In captivity, generally advised to feed 3+ small meals per day of vitamin-enriched mysis, brine shrimp, herbivore pellets, and nori; specimens kept in tanks with insufficient live microfauna or algae growth have been known to emaciate over weeks despite accepting prepared foods.

summary: A peaceful sand-sifting goby with brown vertical bands on a pale body, well regarded for cleaning detritus from fine sand beds in established reef aquaria.
careNotes: Generally considered a beginner-friendly sand-sifter and one of the best aquarium species for maintaining a clean, oxygenated sand bed. Constantly takes mouthfuls of substrate and expels it through the gills, so a fine aragonite sand bed of at least 5 cm depth is generally advised; coarse crushed coral is poorly suited. Active sifting can create sandstorms that settle on nearby corals and irritate sensitive SPS or anemones — placing corals slightly elevated or away from the open sand zone tends to help. Has been reported as an excellent jumper; a tight-fitting lid or mesh top is strongly recommended. Tends to be peaceful with most tankmates but is territorial toward conspecifics and other Amblygobius species; best kept singly or as a male/female pair in tanks under ~400 L. A well-established system with a mature pod population and some film/turf algae growth is generally advised, as specimens in sterile or newly cycled tanks frequently emaciate over time even when accepting prepared foods. Cross-source spread on tank size was wide (LiveAquaria ~110 L / 30 gal; ReefApp 300 L; AquaHoy ~110 L for a pair); 150 L chosen as a cautious middle ground that gives the pair enough sand-bed footprint. Wild-study maximum lifespan was reported at ~14 months but captive specimens are commonly reported to live several years.
breedingNotes: Forms monogamous pairs in the wild; males construct and guard burrows containing demersal egg masses on a roughly semilunar spawning cycle. Captive spawning has been reported occasionally in mature systems but rearing the planktonic larvae is difficult and not routinely accomplished by home aquarists.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Direct product URL returned 404 on fetch; data sourced via WebSearch result snippet of the same LiveAquaria product page (product 206). Confirms 30 gal min tank, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, reef-safe, easy care, jumping risk.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority. Confirms Gobiidae/Gobiiformes, max length 15.0 cm TL, depth 2-20 m (max 52 m), Indo-Pacific distribution, monogamous pair behavior, sand-sifting feeding mode.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-check secondary. Reports 15 cm adult, 300 L minimum, ~26°C, SG 1.026, reef-safe, jumping risk, burrow depth needs body-length + 3 cm — wider tank-size recommendation than retail sources.

additional.name: AquaHoy
additional.notes: Tertiary care guide. Confirms 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, sand bed 5-7 cm minimum, 110 L for a pair, peaceful but territorial with conspecifics, frequent small feedings recommended, wild lifespan ~14 months but multi-year in captivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs form readily and spawn in burrows, but raising the planktonic larvae through metamorphosis is challenging and rarely achieved by home aquarists.
